paper_title: Chemical Composition and Mechanical Properties of Mammoth and Elephant Ivory

methods.description: Process of acquiring and preparing tusk samples from woolly mammoth and African elephant for experimental analysis.

steps.description: The experiments used tusks from woolly mammoth from permafrost and extant African elephant. Both were purchased from the professional ivory carver and authorized dealer Jürgen Schott (Erbach, Germany).
steps.searchable_summary: acquire tusk samples from authorized dealer

steps.description: The elephant samples come from the left tusk of Loxodonta africana. The mammoth samples were taken from a well-preserved Siberian tusk of M. primigenius. Both tusk sections represent seven growth cycles.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare tusk samples representing growth cycles

methods.description: Determination of differences in chemical composition and microstructure of mammoth and elephant ivory using XRD, ICP-OES, SEM, deproteinization, and demineralization.

steps.description: For XRD, CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) and measurement time of one hour per sample were used (2 Theta range 15-72°, step size 0,005°, scan rate c. 1°/min).
steps.searchable_summary: perform XRD analysis on tusk samples

steps.description: The crystal domain size τ in direction of the crystallographic c-axis was calculated using the Scherrer equation with the shape factor K assumed as 0.9, the wavelength λ, the FHWM and the Bragg angle θ of the (002) peak at 2θ = 26°.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate crystal domain size using Scherrer equation

steps.description: ICP-OES samples were prepared by dissolving 50 mg ivory powder in 2 ml of concentrated nitric acid (65 mass-%) for two hours. After dissolving, 8 ml of pure water were added.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare ICP-OES samples by dissolving ivory powder

steps.description: For demineralization, samples with a volume of approx. 300 mm³ were stored in 0.6 M hydrochloric acid. The acid solution was replaced every 24 hours. After seven days, the samples were washed with de-ionized water and freeze-dried for another 24 hours.

For deproteinization, samples with a volume of approx. 300 mm³ were kept in a 2.17 wt% sodium hypochlorite solution for 14 days, which was replaced every 24 hours. The samples were then washed with de-ionized water and dried for 24 hours.

methods.description: Mechanical testing of ivory samples using 3-point bending tests, compression tests, and Vickers hardness tests.

steps.description: Specimens for 3-point bending tests and compression tests had dimensions of 3 mm*4 mm*25 mm and 5 mm*5 mm*10 mm, respectively. The moisture content was 7 mass-%. Samples were taken in two different orientations, longitudinal and transversal.

For 3-point bending tests, 30 longitudinal and 12 transversal samples were used. The samples were cut with a carbide saw blade from the dentine part of the tusks. The samples were then polished before the mechanical test using 1200 grit coated abrasives.

The tests were conducted at room temperature on a Zwick/Roell Z020 machine. The distance between the sample carriers was 16 mm. The pre-load was 0.5 N and loading speed 2 mm/min. The breaking point was determined by the total failure of the samples defined by a 20% decrease in the test load.

To determine the hardness of the ivory, Vickers hardness tests were conducted on polished samples parallel to growth direction with a Vickers pyramid and a Shimadzu HMV-2000 machine. The load was 100 g and load duration was 10 seconds.
